name: Dinosaurs
description: Paleontology terms about the giants that once ruled the Earth.

group.definition: The geological era from about 252 to 66 million years ago, often called the Age of Dinosaurs. It is divided into three periods: the **Triassic**, the **Jurassic** and the **Cretaceous**.
The first period of the **Mesozoic Era** (252–201 million years ago), when the earliest dinosaurs appeared.
The middle period of the **Mesozoic Era** (201–145 million years ago), dominated by giant sauropods and early theropods.
The final period of the **Mesozoic Era** (145–66 million years ago), ending with the mass extinction of non-avian dinosaurs.
The 'lizard-hipped' order of dinosaurs, grouping the **Theropoda** and the long-necked **Sauropodomorpha**.
The 'bird-hipped' order of dinosaurs, made up entirely of herbivores such as the **Ornithopoda**.
A suborder of mostly carnivorous, bipedal **Saurischia** that includes the **Tyrannosauridae** and modern birds.
A suborder of huge, long-necked herbivorous **Saurischia** such as the **Diplodocidae** and **Brachiosauridae**.
A suborder of herbivorous **Ornithischia**, including Iguanodon and the duck-billed **Hadrosauridae**.
A family of large, powerful carnivorous **Theropoda**, most famously Tyrannosaurus rex.
A family of extremely long, slender **Sauropodomorpha** such as Diplodocus and Apatosaurus.
A family of horned, frilled herbivorous **Ornithischia** that includes Triceratops.
A family of plated, spike-tailed herbivorous **Ornithischia**, most famously Stegosaurus.
A family of giant **Sauropodomorpha** with longer front legs and high shoulders, such as Brachiosaurus.
A family of large, semi-aquatic **Theropoda** with crocodile-like snouts and sail-backed bodies, such as Spinosaurus.
A family of heavily armored herbivorous **Ornithischia** with bony plates and a clubbed tail, such as Ankylosaurus.
A family of small, agile feathered **Theropoda** with a sickle-shaped claw, including Velociraptor and Deinonychus.
A family of duck-billed herbivorous **Ornithopoda**, often with elaborate head crests, such as Parasaurolophus.
The preserved remains or traces of ancient organisms, such as bones, eggs or footprints.
A scientist who studies **Fossil**s to understand the history of life on Earth.
The science of naming and classifying organisms into ranked groups such as order, suborder, family, genus and species.
